content: Identify 'P' and 'Q' in the following reaction
Which of the following ion/ions is/are diamagnetic ?
explanation:
To determine which of the given ions are diamagnetic, we need to look at their electronic configurations and the nature of the ligands involved. A diamagnetic substance is one that has all of its electrons paired.

Let's analyze each option one by one:

Option A: $$\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$$

  • Here, the central ion is Co3+.

  • Cobalt's atomic number is 27, so Co3+ has 24 electrons.

  • The electron configuration of Co3+ is given by removing 3 electrons from Co: $$[\text{Ar}] 3d^6$$.

  • Fluoride ($$F^-$$) is a weak field ligand, meaning it does not cause a large split in the d-orbitals.

  • In a weak field, the electron configuration remains as it is without pairing up, thus: $$t_{2g}^4 e_{g}^2$$.

  • Since there are unpaired electrons, the ion $$\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$$ is paramagnetic.

Option B: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$$

  • The central ion here is also Co3+.

  • Similarly, Co3+: $$[\text{Ar}] 3d^6$$.

  • Ammonia ($$\mathrm{NH}_3$$) is a strong field ligand, causing a large split in the d-orbitals which leads to pairing of electrons.

  • In a strong field, the electron configuration can be rearranged to pair up electrons: $$t_{2g}^6 e_{g}^0$$.

  • All electrons are paired.

  • Therefore, the ion $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$$ is diamagnetic.

Option C: $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OH}_2\right)_6\right]^{2+}$$

  • The central ion is Fe2+.

  • Iron's atomic number is 26, so Fe2+ has 24 electrons.

  • The electron configuration of Fe2+ is $$[\text{Ar}] 3d^6$$.

  • Water ($$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$$) is a weak field ligand, similarly not causing a large split.

  • In a weak field, the electron configuration remains: $$t_{2g}^4 e_{g}^2$$ with unpaired electrons.

  • Therefore, the ion $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OH}_2\right)_6\right]^{2+}$$ is paramagnetic.

Option D: $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}$$

  • For Fe2+ in $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}$$.

  • The electron configuration of Fe2+ remains $$[\text{Ar}] 3d^6$$.

  • Cyanide ($$\mathrm{CN}^-$$) is a very strong field ligand, causing a large split leading to pairing.

  • In a strong field situation: $$t_{2g}^6 e_{g}^0$$ with paired electrons.

  • Therefore, the ion $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}$$ is diamagnetic.

Therefore, the diamagnetic ions among the given options are:

  • Option B: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$$
  • Option D: $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}$$

content: Compounds with spin only magnetic moment equivalent to five unpaired electrons are
explanation:
Among the given species

(a) H2O and F are weak field ligands and do not cause pairing of electrons in the central metal atom. As CN is a strong field ligand, it causes pairing of electrons in the central metal atom.

Now,

Oxidation state of :

(b) Fe in [Fe(H2O)6]Cl3 = (+) 3 and electronic configuration of Fe3+ ions is 3d5 4s0 Fe3+ =


i.e. have 5 unpaired electrons.

(c) Fe in K3[FeF6]

Oxidation no. of Fe = (+) 3

electronic configuration of Fe3+ is 3d5 4s0


i.e. have 5 unpaired electrons.

(d) Oxidation state of Mn in K4[MnF6] = (+) 2 and electronic configuration of Mn2+ is 3d5 4s0


i.e., have 5 unpaired electrons.

Hence, options (b), (c) and (d) are the correct options.

content: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
explanation:
Incorrect statement is given in option (d).

In $$\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$$ complex, $$\mathrm{Co}$$ is in +3 oxidation state.

$$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^6 4 s^0$$

Also, $$\mathrm{F}^{-}$$ is a weak field ligand. So, no pairing of electron will takes place. Thus, the compound is paramagnetic with 4 unpaired electrons instead of 2 .

content: The calculated spin-only magnetic moment values in BM for $$\mathrm{[FeCl_4]^-}$$ and $$\mathrm{[Fe(CN)_6]^{3-}}$$ are
explanation:
For $$\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_4\right]^{-}$$

$$\mathrm{Fe}$$ is in +3 oxidation state. Thus,

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5 4 s^0$$

In this complex,

$$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$$ is a weak field ligand. So, no pairing of electron will take place. Thus, it has 5 unpaired electrons $$(n=5)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mu & =\sqrt{n(n+2)} \mathrm{BM}=\sqrt{5(5+2)} \mathrm{~BM} \\ & =5.9 \mathrm{~BM} \end{aligned}$$

Now, for $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$$

$$\mathrm{Fe}$$ is in +3 oxidation state and $$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$$ is a strong field ligand. So, pairing of electron will takes place.

Thus, $$n=1$$

$$\therefore \quad \mu=\sqrt{1(1+2)}=1.732 \mathrm{~BM}$$

content: The correct statement about the magnetic properties of $${\left[ {Fe{{(CN)}_6}} \right]^{3 - }}$$ and $${\left[ {Fe{F_6}} \right]^{3 - }}$$ is
explanation:
In both the compounds $$\mathrm{Fe}$$ is in +3 oxidation state. Thus,

$$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5 4 s^0$$

Now, for $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$$

Pairing of electron will takes place due to presence of strong field ligand i.e $$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$$ but has one unpaired electron thus paramagnetic.

Now, for $$\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{3-}$$,

$$\mathrm{F}^{-}$$ is a weak field ligand. So, no pairing of electron, thus has five unpaired electrons. Therefore, paramagnetic.

content: Nickel combines with a uninegative monodentate ligand (X$$^-$$) to form a paramagnetic complex [NiX$$_4$$]$$^{2-}$$. The hybridisation involved and number of unpaired electrons present in the complex are respectively
explanation:
In the given complex $$\left[\mathrm{Ni} X_4\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{Ni}$$ is present in +2 oxidation state. Thus, it configuration will be $$\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8 4 s^0$$

Thus, the given compound is paramagnetic with two unpaired electrons and $$s p^3$$ hybridisation.

The formula of sodium nitroprusside is $$\mathrm{Na_2[Fe(CN)_5NO]}$$.

Hence, option (c) is correct.

content: Choose the correct statement for the [Ni(CN)4]2$$-$$ complex ion (Atomic no. of Ni = 28)

options.content: The complex is square planar and paramagnetic.
The complex is tetrahedral and diamagnetic.
The complex is square planar and diamagnetic.
The complex is tetrahedral and paramagnetic.

explanation:
$$\mathrm{Ni^{2+}}$$ ion has $$\mathrm{3d^{8}}$$ outer electronic configuration. $$\mathrm{CN^{-}}$$ is a strong field ligand. So, pairing of electron takes place.

Electronic configuration of $$\mathrm{[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}}$$

Therefore, $$\mathrm{[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}}$$ is $$dsp^2$$ hybridisation. As all the electrons are paired, hence it is diamagnetic. Geometry of $$\mathrm{[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}}$$ is square planar.

Hence, option (c) is correct.

content: A solution is made by a concentrated solution of Co(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub> with a concentrated solution of NaNO<sub>2</sub> is 50% acetic acid. A solution of a salt containing metal M is added to the mixture, when a yellow precipitate is formed. Metal 'M' is
explanation:
The given reaction is as follows:

$$Co{(N{O_3})_2} + 7N{a_2}N{O_2} + 4C{H_3}COOH \to \mathop {N{a_3}[Co{{(N{O_2})}_6}]}\limits_{Sodium\,cobalt\,nitrile} + 3N{O_2} + 2{H_2}O + 4C{H_3}COONa$$

With the salt of potassium, sodium cobalt nitrile forms insoluble double salt K2Na[Co(NO2)6] . H2O. This salt is yellow colored ppt.

content: Indicate the number of unpaired electrons in K<sub>3</sub>[Fe(CN)<sub>6</sub>] and K<sub>4</sub>[Fe(CN)<sub>6</sub>].
explanation:
In K3[Fe(CN)6]Fe present in +3 oxidation state outer electronic configuration of Fe3+ =


As CN is a strong field ligand. So, it causes the pairing of electrons.

Therefore, K3[Fe(CN)6] =


Thus, in this complex, number of unpaired electron is one.

In K4[Fe(CN)6]Fe, present in +2 oxidation state outer electronic configuration of Fe2+


As in this case also the ligand is CN (which is a strong field). So, it again causes the pairing of the electron. This can be shown in following way.

K4[Fe(CN)6] =


Thus, in this case the number of unpaired electron is zero.

content: Which of the following compounds have magnetic moment identical with [Cr(H<sub>2</sub>O)<sub>6</sub>]<sup>3+</sup> ?
explanation:
When two complexes have same number of unpaired electrons, they have same value of magnetic moment. The number of unpaired electrons present in [Cu(H2O)6]2+, [Mn(H2O)6]4+, [Mn(H2O)6]3+, [Fe(H2O)6]3+ and [Cr(H2O)6]3+ are 0, 4, 3, 5 and 3 respectively.

Thus, the magnetic moment of [Mn(H2O)6]3+ and [Cr(H2O)6]3+ are identical as they have same number of unpaired electrons (i.e., 3).

content: Indicate the correct IUPAC name of the coordination compound shown in the figure.
explanation:
Let the oxidation state of central atom (Cr) be x.

Then,

(x $$ \times $$ 1) + (4 $$ \times $$ 0) + (2 $$ \times $$ $$ - $$1) = +1

x $$ - $$ 2 = +1

x = +3

Thus, the correct IUPAC name of complex is

cis-tetraamminedichlorochromium (III) chloride.

So, the correct answer is (d).

content: The conductivity measurement of a coordination compound of cobalt (III) shows that it dissociates into 3 ions in solution. The compound is
explanation:
Co-ordinate compound of Co (III) dissociates into 3 ions in the solution means it has two ionisable-ions out side the coordination sphere with oxidation state of cobalt (Co) = +3

In option (a)

Name : Hexaammine cobalt (III) chloride

Formula : [Co(NH3)6]Cl3

$$\because$$ Oxidation state of Co = (+) 3

In option (b)

Name : Pentaammine sulphatocobalt (III) chloride

Formula : [Co(NH3)5(SO4)]Cl

$$\because$$ Oxidation state of Co = +3

In option (c)

Name : Pentaamminechloridocobalt (III) sulphate

Formula : [Co(NH3)5Cl]SO4

$$\because$$ Oxidation state of Co = + 3

In option (d)

Name : Pentaamminechloridocobalt (III) chloride

Formula : [Co(NH3)5Cl]Cl2

$$\because$$ Oxidation state of Co = +3

Dissociation of this compound is shown below :

$$[CO{(N{H_3})_5}Cl]C{l_2} \to \underbrace {CO{{(N{H_3})}_5}Cl{]^{2 + }} + 2C{l^ - }}_{3\,ions}$$

compound can give total 3-ions, as follows. one [Co(NH3)5Cl]2+ and two Cl$$-$$ ions.

Hence, option (d) is the correct answer.

content: The main reason that SiCl<sub>4</sub> is easily hydrolysed as compared to CCl<sub>4</sub> is that

options.content: Si $$-$$ Cl bond is weaker than C $$-$$ Cl bond
Si can extend its coordination number beyond four

explanation:
The main reason that SiCl4 is easily hydrolysed as compared to CCl4 is that Si can extend its coordination number beyond four because it has vacant d-orbital. On the other hand, carbon has no d-orbital thus, it cannot extend its coordination number beyond four, so its halides are not attacked (hydrolysed) by water. The vacant d-orbitals of Si can coordinate with water molecules and hence their halides are hydrolysed by water.

The hydrolysis of SiCl4 occurs due to coordination of $$-$$OH with empty 3d-orbitals of Si-atom of SiCl4 molecule.

content: Silver chloride dissolves in excess of ammonium hydroxide solution. The cation present in the resulting solution is
explanation:
Silver chloride dissolves in excess of ammonium hydroxide solution. The cation present in the resulting solution is $${[Ag{(N{H_3})_2}]^ + }$$. In aqueous solution, silver chloride exist as Ag+ and Cl$$-$$. Ag+ present in solution reacts with NH3 to form $${[Ag{(N{H_3})_2}]^ + }$$.

$$A{g^ + }C{l^ - } + 2N{H_3} \to {[Ag{(N{H_3})_2}]^ + }C{l^ - }$$

content: Ferric ion forms a Prussian blue precipitate due to the formation of
explanation:
Ferric ion forms a Prussian blue precipitate due to the formation of $$F{e_4}{[Fe{(CN)_6}]_3}$$. This complex is formed during the determine action of presence of nitrogen in the given sample. In this method, to portion of sodium fusion extract, freshly prepared ferrous sulphate, FeSO4 solution is added and warmed. Then about 2 to 3 drops of FeCl3 solution are added and acidified with conc. HCl. The appearance of a Prussian blue colour indicate the presence of nitrogen.

$$FeS{O_4} + 2NaOH \to Fe{(OH)_2} + N{a_2}S{O_4}$$

$$6NaCN + Fe{(OH)_2} \to \mathop {N{a_4}[Fe{{(CN)}_6}]}\limits_{\text{Sodium ferrocyanide}} + 2NaOH$$

$$3N{a_4}[Fe{(CN)_6}] + 4FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {F{e_4}{{[Fe{{(CN)}_6}]}_3}}\limits_{\text{Ferric ferrocyanide (prussian blue)}} + 12NaCl$$

content: The number of unpaired electrons in $${[NiC{l_4}]^{2 - }}$$, $$Ni{(CO)_4}$$ and $${[Cu{(N{H_3})_4}]^{2 + }}$$ respectively are
explanation:
Configuration for metal (M) in various complex


Ni2+ has 2 unpaired electrons


Ni0 has zero unpaired electrons with sp3-hybridisation.


Cu2+ has one unpaired electron with dsp2-hybridisation.

$$\therefore$$ (b) is the correct answer, i.e.,

Number of unpaired electron (2, 0, 1)
